speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 22, 1970-- 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Johanna Kimmel Goldberg, maiden name Schmiedl. I'm Yvonne Walter, and this is San Francisco, California, United States of America. The language is English. My name is-- my name is Yvonne Walter. Today is August 22, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Johanna Kimmel Goldberg, in San Francisco, California, United States of America. The language is English. Good morning. Would you please state and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Johanna Kimmel Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I spell it? J-O-H-A-N-N-A K-I-M-M-E-L G-O-L-D-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schmiedl, S-C-H-M-I-E-D-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 84. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you told me a little about your childhood, about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gladly. I had a very protected childhood. My parents were wealthy. My mother was born, in this time, a liberated woman. A very rare thing. Because before she married, she went to a school where they teach women to make money professionally. And that was very rare, because in this time, women had only to be mothers and housewives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school and where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was [GERMAN]. I'm sorry. It's German, and it's very hard to translate it. It's very hard to translate it. My father comes from a wealthy family. They had a ranch and sawmills out of Vienna. The town is called [INAUDIBLE] now. And it is near the Monastery of Melk, M-E-L-K. And my father went to school there because it was the only gymnasium in the neighborhood. And there were three sons, and they sent them. In summer, we had a carriage. In the winter, with [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sled.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slide, yeah. And that was it. And I had a very nice childhood. Very protected. We made trips. I remember, we spent every spring and autumn in the Riviera. And in the meantime, in the time in between-- as it was, you just-- you made the [INAUDIBLE] in different spas. And then one evening, when my-- my mother went to [NON-ENGLISH], I met my future husband. Years, years later. [LAUGHS] And my father was not very happy with him, because my father was a proud Viennese man. And to marry a German subject was the last thing in the world. And he said, there's only one explanation. When you met him, he-- revolving the door of the hotel. He was-- he was thrown over-- over to your head. And this really explains everything. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name-- his name-- my father was Richard [? Josef ?] Schmiedl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe a little bit more about his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was partly a vice president of the [NON-ENGLISH] And then later, he left his office and was buying plots and built houses and-- and sold them. And I think he made nice money with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say the name of his--

speaker: interviewee
text: One house he never sold. And this house is a very funny thing. He hasn't sold it because the roof was finished on the day of my birth. In Vienna, when a roof is finished, there's a big party. And it was exactly the date of my birth. So he never sold this house, and I fought for this house later, after the war, because it was stolen of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother and her work?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] My mother was bored being a housewife. She was-- opened a big store of shoes, as my father was anyway a silent companion in a few shoes factories. So it worked all perfect together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to elementary school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I had-- there's-- I would say the first experience with anti-Semitism, because when I was eight years old, I had a very dangerous surgery. What is called a [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Should I try to translate it? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in this time, of course, a surgery like this was much more difficult than it is today. And this was a very dangerous point, so my head was fixed here the whole time of surgery, in some blockage, in a machine, that I couldn't move. Because the tiniest movement would be dangerous. So I was already healthy, and I went to school, and there was, here, still blue. And one day, my teacher-- I still remember her name-- [? Margareta ?] [? Liebl. ?] She said-- I was eight years old-- oh, our nice [? Hanzi ?] Schmiedl has-- my pet name was [? Hanzi-- ?] has forgotten to wash her neck today. And I started to cry, and [INAUDIBLE] running home. My father took my hand and said, we arrange it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went immediately to the headmaster of the school and said, what do you think about it? He said, oh, that's awful. I'm devastated. But would [? Hanzi ?] be satisfied when the teacher makes an apology in front of the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried. I didn't answer. My father said, yes, she would. And that was done. And from this time, the teacher behaved marvelous. [LAUGHS] That was the elementary school. And then I-- when I-- when I was 10, I entered a very well-known gymnasium in Vienna. It is one of the most severe schools, and it is only for girls. And I had, for instance, Latin every day for eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you name that school, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It is [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I-- as I wanted to explain, I had Latin every day because my father insisted when you have a true knowledge of Latin, you understand a lot. And that's true. Even a lot of English words have-- have origins in Latin. And still today, I know Latin poetry by heart. You'll never forget a thing like that when you have it every day eight years. And in the last vacation, I went with my mother to one of the spas that I mentioned before, [? Calspot. ?] And in the hotel where we lived, lived a man from Germany, with his mother, who accompanied his mother to the spa. And that was my future husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I finished school, and my father was very, very angry that he called every day from Berlin. He said, my daughter is still a schoolgirl! It's awful, this! Only a German could do a thing like that. You see? And it's very sad that a Viennese girl couldn't find a boyfriend in Vienna. She had to import one. So he was mad. Anyway-- Can we go back just a little bit about some of your earlier experiences in Vienna, as far as your Jewish identity, the Shabbot, if you celebrated that as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I lived in a very assimilated household. So I had no Jewish connection. I was-- was later a Girl Scout in a-- a Zionistic movement, but my parents really didn't live as Jews. I still remember the [INAUDIBLE] time for Christmas. My husband-- my father said to his bookkeeper, I want the same amount for the Social Democrats for the [INAUDIBLE] for the Jews. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I mean, I had really no Jewish experience whatsoever. We-- I-- in the school, I knew I was Jewish, and I even was in some way, very attracted to a lot of things, but not influenced by my parents. What kind of things attracted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The stories, for instance. I loved the Pesach story, and I loved the Purim story. What a young girl would like, you see. But my-- my parents had no idea what it is about. My mother had a faint idea. My husband-- my father, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about some of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had mostly gentile friends, not Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of your hobbies or extracurricular lessons as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child? I was always making sketches. I was good at drawing. I liked to swim. And that-- and what-- what a young girl doesn't. And I even liked school. [LAUGHS] And we had a lot of sports at school. Obligatory. Obligatory? Yeah. Skiing and skating, and I always joined. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Vienna, at your time, toward the Jews, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you-- you didn't mention that you are Jewish. No one asked you. And for myself, I can say, I knew I was Jewish, but I was never proud and never ashamed of it. It was quite natural, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm an only child. I had only dogs, always, because I was brought up with dogs, and I spent vacations on the ranch of my grandparents. And so there were horses and dogs and cats. And it was very funny. In the home of my grandparents, where every Saturday night, chamber music and the animals were allowed to be present. The animals only couldn't-- were not permitted to go to the dining room, as my grandfather said, as long as they are not using cutlery, they don't go to the dining room. And I loved being there. Those were beautiful surroundings. Even Brahms was a friend of my grandfather, and he came sometimes to concerts and said he could feel at home. Then named his-- one of the cats Clara. And you know why, because his love was Clara from Schumann. Clara Schumann was a love of Brahms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to the cook, what a funny name, Clara, for a cat. Why? She said, a child has not to ask questions. [LAUGHS] That was it, and it was really a lovely childhood. Can you tell me how-- how many years you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the elementary school, eight years. And then I graduated and got immediately married and immediately pregnant. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little about the name of your husband and what he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband was a freelance journalist. And as he-- won't-- my-- my father said, the condition is that you live in Vienna with my daughter. I don't give permission that she go to Germany. What will you do for a living? My husband said, you will see. And my father said, I have to tell you, my daughter is a poor girl. She doesn't get any dowry. Nothing. Not one shilling. He said, I'm sorry. I haven't asked you. [LAUGHS] And that's OK with me. And I got as a wedding gift a beautiful villa, completed with carpets and silver and that-- and china. What everyone needs. Fully furnished, of course. But my father insisted that everything go in my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was some pre-marriage agreement made that notary said whatever is in the house is only property of [INAUDIBLE] Hanzi. That's it. And my husband said he didn't mind. As long as you let me sleep in your bed. It's not-- it's OK. And that was it. And then I got married on the 9th of November, '30. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18. And my father had a funny idea. He said, only nouveau riche takes their daughter to the synagogue. I don't do it. And he ordered from [? Chambrun, ?] from the castle, the carriages with Lipizzaner. I'm so sorry that all these photos got lost during moving and emigrating, because we had lovely photos. How these Lipizzaners-- you now have seen them, possibly, here too. With the carriages, took me to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you at synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the synagogue service for you, since you grew up without--

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally. Normally. Only one thing I insisted. In Vienna, it was used-- they're used when the bride comes in to play [GERMAN]. Do you know? [NON-ENGLISH] It's a melody of Richard Wagner. And I insisted I don't want it. It looks-- it's for me to set-- I insisted on the 'HaTikvah.' And it was a very new idea, and I the rabbi didn't like it. But I was stubborn, and I said, it's my wedding, and I want to be happy on my wedding day. And so the 'HaTikvah' was played.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this, it's not like here, that the synagogues have names. It is only where they are located.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pursue your Jewish life after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, my husband came, really, from a religious home. Yeah? But we left. We had a normal household, not kosher. Because I told you, I was a stubborn girl. And I said, possibly I'm wrong, but I don't see a connection between soul and digestion. And I think the laws where hygienic laws, and they're-- today's is a situation, another one. And we didn't fight about it. We lived in a very comfortable house. Life. After nine months, my daughter was born. We had a nurse for the child, and we had the cook and a girl to make the rooms. So I really-- I think till Hitler came, I was not rinsing a teacup. [LAUGHS] My mother used to say, don't go in the kitchen. The kitchen air has a smell. Goes in the hair. You'll never get rid of it. Don't. So I didn't. But anyway, I like to eat, and I always do, till now. And then when you like to eat, you are interested in cooking. So I bought cookbooks, you will see, as a young wife. And I got the cook of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, [? Lucia, ?] come to the kitchen. And I watch you, but don't tell mother. [LAUGHS] And so I really learned nicely to cook and to bake. Could you tell me a little about your husband's work and what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He worked later only for German publicity firms, and he worked very successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened during the years you were married, 1930 to 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from '30, I say, till '38. In the meantime, '31, my father passed away. And as we had, really, a large villa with a guestroom, we decided mother should live with us. So she lived with us. It was really, sometimes harmonic, sometimes not. Like it is with every mother-in-law and her son-in-law. But it worked out in the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one-- could I continue now already for Nazi Germany, Nazi Austria? When Hitler invaded Austria, I had the feeling the world is falling apart. From one day to the other, the-- in the windows of the apartments, the pictures of Schuschnigg were taken out and there will be pictures of Hitler there, put in. And I was so disgusted.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew already. I met a few-- a few Austrians and politicians. And I knew that the Austrians were not flexible in their political opinion. They were always firm for one party, for the majority. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that explains everything. And this explains how quickly they agreed to Hitler. Because they saw that as possibly a prosperity coming, and the majority was for Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- we really didn't know how it will work out. It was all so unclear. And my mother said, it will not last long, because the [NON-ENGLISH] do you know what that means? Wouldn't agree to it. So Hitler would have to leave. It can't go on. He's a stranger. No, no. That would-- don't be nervous, she told to me. Everything will be all right. Our life wouldn't be changed. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '38. When Hitler came to Austria. But we met a few acquaintances, and everyone said to emigrate. And for instance, my mother had a very beautiful property. And [INAUDIBLE], who was a friend of hers, says, you know I have enough of America. You could change this property for a big property in Long Island. And because you couldn't take money out any way, but this could be still done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, I shall think it over. And she spoke with Emily and with other people. And she said, you know, everyone told me, what should I do there in America with the natives? Because she had a very primitive idea that it is not Vienna. Anyway, [SIGHS] life went on. And one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she watched-- my husband was with his car-- drive in. Yes? And some uniformed men took him away with the car. I-- she watched it from her window, and she told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, possibly it's some information they want. You know how it is nowadays. I said, well, he will come back. And he didn't come back in one day, in two days. He didn't come back in one week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know where he is and what happened to him. And after one week, I went to the [GERMAN] and said, you know, my husband was taken away from our drive in with his car. What happened to him? He said, we sent him on a vacation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was it. Let's say, approximately two weeks later, I got a card from my husband. I'm in Dachau, in the concentration camp. And I can write one postcard a month. And you can send me every month, I don't remember, it was 12 or 15 shillings. A bagatelle. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this one sentence immensely convinced me it wouldn't be a longer stay. It wouldn't be that they sent him home immediately. And I thought what I should do, and I really had no idea what to do. [DRINKS] My husband had a cousin. And this cousin was in this time the managing director of the [GERMAN] in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to, you know, to my mother, you stay here, and I'll go to Berlin, and I'll speak with Alfred. In his position, he would know what to do. So I went to Berlin, and he had really a position-- he was on good terms with the Nazi regime, because he was the managing director for immigration to Palestine. So he said, darling, you can't do anything. Now is [GERMAN]. How to translate. The camp is closed. And no one can go out. So you can't do anything. I said-- he said, now what would you expect me to do? He said, take your daughter and go to Palestine. I-- it was-- Palestine was our only possibility. I would like-- I really wanted to go to the states. But the quota for Vienna was very bad. That means we had to wait three years. In three years, we could be a few times dead already. So-- and in Palestine, you could not come in legally, but if you had the capitalism certificate. We couldn't have it because in the moment, everything was blocked. The bank account was blocked, and I was only allowed to take a certain amount out for monthly living. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the brother of this Palestine manager was a very famous gynecologist in Israel, a professor. And he made a deposit at the government of Palestine for 1,000 pounds in the name of my husband and me, and we got a capitalism certificate, and I was informed my certificate is in the Palestine and British consulate in Geneva.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but it didn't help me. So he said, you take your daughter and go to Palestine. And they said, I asked him, and who would take care of Bob? His nickname-- pet name was Bob. He said, God will help. God will know. I had all confidence in-- in God, you see. But it was really no-- I wouldn't say a date. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I thought, what should I do? And I lived with the-- with the aunt of my husband. And she wanted to relax me. She said, we think it over. Today, I take you to a coffee shop in [INAUDIBLE], and I buy you a nice cake. So we went to this coffee shop. And on the adjoining table, I saw three guys in a conversation, and they mentioned a few things about Dachau and Gestapo and hope. And you see, I was 26, so very young. And I got up, and I said, I beg your pardon? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say bad times rectify bad manners. I was hearing what you are saying. My husband is in Dachau. Could you help? And one guy, an elderly guy, says, give me his card, yeah? And he said, not here. Come tomorrow to this office. 4 o'clock. And he stopped speaking with me. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went home with my aunt, and I said, tomorrow, I shall see. And I went there, and he told me. He said, I can't give you much hope, and I can't help. And I don't charge you, because I don't do anything for you. I can only tell you, there is one guy in the [GERMAN], an old count. His room number, I guess, is 760 or something. I don't remember the number now. And if you are successful to see him and to convince him, possibly you have hope.
